ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM (Vegetable dishes) used as the closest defensible category; no specific kurma or korma code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for vegetable kurma or vegetable korma as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of vegetable kurma is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one medium bowl as commonly served alongside rice or roti in a Singaporean or South Indian meal.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable Kurma — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g values. Values are accepted as-is. Saturated fat estimated from coconut milk contribution (~4.8 g/100g) consistent with HPB data for coconut-milk-based vegetable curries; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat not reported by HPB for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — mixed vegetable curry / legume-vegetable stew analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetable kurma in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 45 is estimated from the closest analogous entries: mixed vegetable curry with legumes (GI ~45–50, low category) reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as (45 × 12.8 × 2.0) / 100 = 11.5 g. The input record cited GI 15 from an 'Embedded GI Database' which is not an approved source; that value has been replaced with this defensible analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (143 mg/100g) and high dietary fibre (5.0 g/100g) consistent with DASH dietary principles
hypertension.guidance: Vegetable kurma is suitable for individuals with hypertension due to its low sodium content of 143 mg/100g and high fibre of 5.0 g/100g; however, the saturated fat from coconut milk (~4.8 g/100g) should be moderated by requesting a reduced-coconut-milk preparation and avoiding additional salt at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (12.8 g/100g), high dietary fibre (5.0 g/100g), low sugar (2.8 g/100g), and estimated low GI (~45) producing a moderate glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Vegetable kurma is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in a 150–200 g portion alongside a low-GI carbohydrate such as brown rice or wholegrain roti, as the high fibre content attenuates postprandial glucose response and the estimated glycemic load per 200 g serving is approximately 11.5 g.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from coconut milk (~4.8 g/100g) is the primary cardiovascular concern, partially offset by anti-inflammatory spices (turmeric, ginger) and high dietary fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume vegetable kurma in moderate portions (150 g), preferring preparations with reduced coconut milk to limit saturated fat intake, while benefiting from the anti-inflammatory curcumin and ginger content and the cholesterol-lowering effect of 5.0 g/100g dietary fibre.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spice profile (turmeric curcumin, ginger) and high dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Vegetable kurma is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis given its turmeric and ginger content; portion should be limited to ~150 g during active flares to minimise saturated fat load from coconut milk, and individuals sensitive to nightshade vegetables (potato, tomato) should monitor personal tolerance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable kurma (also spelled vegetable korma) is an Indian-origin vegetable stew widely consumed in Singapore and Malaysia as part of everyday South Indian and Malay-Indian cuisine. It is prepared by simmering a mixture of diced potatoes, carrots, tomatoes, french beans, and green peas in a creamy gravy made from thick coconut milk and water, seasoned with a blend of aromatic spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, ginger, and garlic. The dish has a mild to moderately spiced flavour profile with a rich, slightly sweet coconut-milk base and a soft, tender vegetable texture. It is fully vegetarian and vegan, gluten-free, and halal. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 123 kcal, 1.7 g protein, 7.2 g fat (predominantly saturated from coconut milk at approximately 4.8 g), 12.8 g carbohydrate, 2.8 g sugar, 5.0 g dietary fibre, and 143 mg sodium. The high fibre content supports glycaemic control and gut health. The estimated glycemic index is approximately 45 (low category), making it suitable for blood glucose management when portioned appropriately. Clinically, it is considered low concern for hypertension and Type 2 Diabetes, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to its saturated fat content from coconut milk. The anti-inflammatory spices turmeric and ginger provide additional benefit for inflammatory conditions suchs as rheumatoid arthritis. It is commonly served as a side dish with rice, roti canai, or chapati in Singaporean hawker centres, Indian restaurants, and home cooking.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from coconut milk composition consistent with HPB data for similar dishes. GI replaced from non-approved embedded source with a defensible analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables.
notes: 1. Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB for this entry): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrients, and moisture data are not reported by HPB Singapore Foodo for this vegetable kurma entry as of 2026. 2. Saturated fat (4.8 g/100g) is estimated from the known fatty acid profile of thick coconut milk at the proportion consistent with 7.2 g total fat in a coconut-milk-based vegetable curry; this is consistent with HPB data for analogous dishes. 3. The input record cited GI 15 from an 'Embedded GI Database' which is not an approved source under Project Veritas v2 source policy; this value has been replaced with GI 45 estimated from the closest analogous entry (mixed vegetable curry with legumes) in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. 4. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine category: English (food_name), Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi. 5. All core macros (protein_g 1.7, fat_g 7.2, carbohydrate_g 12.8) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification required.
